title: Advanced Diction & Commonly Confused Words II
summary: A broad reference to the higher-frequency confusable word pairs, idioms, and prepositions that distinguish polished diplomatic writing from merely competent prose.
content:
DTOT editing items punish writers who reach for the word that *sounds* right. Many traps are near-homophones that mean different things. Master these by meaning, not by ear.

Verbs vs. nouns and easy slips:
• accept (to receive) vs. except (to exclude): "The mission will accept all delegates except observers."
• advice (noun) vs. advise (verb): "She advised caution; her advice was sound."
• elicit (to draw out) vs. illicit (illegal): "The interview elicited details of illicit transfers."
• precede (come before) vs. proceed (go forward): "The toast precedes the dinner; we may now proceed."
• lead (present tense / the metal) vs. led (past tense): "She led the talks last year; she will lead them again."
• loose (not tight) vs. lose (to misplace): "Do not lose the loose pages of the brief."

Meaning-distinction pairs:
• allusion (indirect reference) vs. illusion (false impression): "His allusion to 1989 created the illusion of consensus."
• continual (repeated) vs. continuous (uninterrupted): "Continual interruptions delayed the continuous broadcast."
• disinterested (impartial) vs. uninterested (bored): "A mediator must be disinterested, not uninterested."
• imply (sender suggests) vs. infer (receiver concludes): "The note implied delay; we inferred a problem."
• stationary (not moving) vs. stationery (writing paper): "The motorcade was stationary; the letter was on embassy stationery."

Quantity and comparison words:
• among (three or more) vs. between (two, or pairwise relations): "The accord was reached between the two states, among many parties."
• amount (uncountable) vs. number (countable): "A large number of files but a small amount of data."
• farther (physical distance) vs. further (degree/extent): "We drove farther north and discussed the matter no further."
• than (comparison) vs. then (time/sequence): "The summit lasted longer than expected; then it adjourned."

Logic and structure words:
• comprise (the whole comprises the parts; never "is comprised of") vs. compose (parts compose the whole): "The union comprises 27 states" = "27 states compose the union."
• lay (transitive, needs an object: lay/laid/laid) vs. lie (intransitive, no object: lie/lay/lain): "Lay the report on the desk; let the matter lie."
• that (restrictive, no comma — defines which one) vs. which (nonrestrictive, set off by commas — adds extra detail): "The clause that we drafted survived. The clause, which ran three pages, was cut."
• e.g. (for example) vs. i.e. (that is): "core staff (e.g., analysts)" vs. "the senior post, i.e., the chief of mission."

Idiom and preposition fixed pairs, plus jargon: write "different from" (not "different than"), "capable of," "comply with," "consists of," "prefer X to Y," "responsible for." Cut empty jargon ("leverage synergies," "going forward," "at this point in time" → "now") in favor of plain, exact words.
keyPoints: accept = receive; except = exclude. advice = noun; advise = verb.
elicit = draw out; illicit = illegal. precede = come before; proceed = go forward.
continual = repeated with breaks; continuous = uninterrupted.
disinterested = impartial; uninterested = bored. imply = suggest (speaker); infer = conclude (listener).
among = 3+; between = 2 or pairwise. amount = uncountable; number = countable.
farther = distance; further = degree/extent. than = comparison; then = time.
comprise: the whole comprises the parts — never 'is comprised of'.
lay needs an object (lay it down); lie does not (lie down). Past of lie = lay; past of lay = laid.
that = restrictive (no commas); which = nonrestrictive (commas). e.g. = for example; i.e. = that is.
Use fixed idioms: different from, capable of, comply with, prefer X to Y; cut jargon and wordiness.

flashcards.front: accept vs. except
flashcards.back: accept = to receive/agree; except = to exclude/leave out. 'All accepted the terms except one.'

flashcards.front: Correct: 'Please advice the team on next steps.'
flashcards.back: 'Please advise the team.' Advise = verb; advice = noun.

flashcards.front: elicit vs. illicit
flashcards.back: elicit = to draw out (a response); illicit = illegal/forbidden. 'elicit testimony about illicit funds.'

flashcards.front: continual vs. continuous
flashcards.back: continual = repeated, with breaks; continuous = uninterrupted, unbroken in time/space.

flashcards.front: disinterested vs. uninterested
flashcards.back: disinterested = impartial, no stake; uninterested = bored, indifferent. Judges should be disinterested.

flashcards.front: imply vs. infer
flashcards.back: The speaker/writer implies (suggests); the listener/reader infers (concludes). They are opposite ends.

flashcards.front: among vs. between
flashcards.back: between = two parties (or pairwise among several); among = three or more as a group.

flashcards.front: amount vs. number
flashcards.back: amount = uncountable mass (amount of work); number = countable items (number of reports).

flashcards.front: farther vs. further
flashcards.back: farther = physical distance; further = figurative degree/extent or 'in addition'.

flashcards.front: Correct: 'is comprised of'
flashcards.back: Wrong idiom. Use 'comprises' (whole comprises parts) or 'is composed of'. 'The board comprises nine members.'

flashcards.front: lay vs. lie
flashcards.back: lay = put down (needs object): lay/laid/laid. lie = recline (no object): lie/lay/lain.

flashcards.front: that vs. which
flashcards.back: that = restrictive, defines, no commas. which = nonrestrictive, adds extra info, commas.

flashcards.front: e.g. vs. i.e.
flashcards.back: e.g. = for example (a sample); i.e. = that is (a restatement/clarification).

flashcards.front: stationary vs. stationery
flashcards.back: stationary = not moving; stationery = writing paper (think 'e' for envelope).

flashcards.front: than vs. then; loose vs. lose
flashcards.back: than = comparison; then = time/sequence. loose = not tight; lose = to misplace/fail to keep.

flashcards.front: Idiom check: 'different than', 'comply to'
flashcards.back: Use 'different from' and 'comply with'. Also: capable of, prefer X to Y, responsible for.

title: Sentence Correction & Editing Strategy
summary: A systematic checklist-and-elimination method for 'identify the error' and 'choose the best revision' items, with the trap patterns that catch unwary test-takers.
content:
Sentence-correction items are won by method, not by feel. The sentence that 'sounds right' is often the trap. Run a fixed scan, then eliminate by rule.

The error scan (in order): read the whole sentence first, then check —
• Agreement: subject-verb and pronoun-antecedent. Watch for words between subject and verb. "The list of demands was rejected" (subject = list).
• Pronouns: case (who/whom, I/me) and clear reference. "Each delegate cast his or her vote" (not 'their' if singular is required).
• Tense: consistent and logical in sequence. "By the time we arrived, the session had ended."
• Modifiers: every opening modifier must attach to the right noun. "Reviewing the file, the analyst found an error" (not 'the error found the analyst').
• Parallelism: items in a series/comparison share form. "The post requires drafting, briefing, and negotiating."
• Comparison: compare like to like, completely. "Our budget is larger than Spain's" (not 'than Spain').
• Idiom: fixed prepositions and standard expressions (comply with, different from).

The elimination rules for 'best revision': cross out any choice that (1) introduces a new grammatical error, (2) is wordy or redundant, (3) changes the intended meaning, (4) shifts tone (slang in a formal memo), or (5) is needlessly passive. What survives is usually the answer.

Trap patterns to memorize:
• Longer is not better. Test-writers add padding ("due to the fact that," "in order to," "there are several reports that indicate") to disguise a weaker choice. The concise option is usually correct.
• Passive padding: "A decision was made by the committee" → "The committee decided."
• False 'sounds right': comma splices and 'between you and I' sound natural but are wrong ('between you and me').
• Trap of completeness: an answer that fixes one error while adding another.

Combining and splitting: combine choppy sentences with subordination to show the real relationship — "The talks stalled. Both sides left." → "When the talks stalled, both sides left." Split an overloaded run-on into clear units. Subordinate the less important idea; keep the main point in the main clause.

Transitions and logical connectors signal the relationship between ideas — choose by logic, not habit: however / nevertheless / nonetheless = contrast; therefore / consequently / thus = result; moreover / furthermore = addition; for example / specifically = illustration; meanwhile / subsequently = time. A wrong connector ('however' where you mean 'therefore') reverses the logic of an otherwise correct sentence.
keyPoints: Read the whole sentence first; then run a fixed scan: agreement, pronoun, tense, modifier, parallelism, comparison, idiom.
Find the true subject by ignoring words between it and the verb ('The set of files IS ready').
Eliminate any choice that adds an error, adds words, changes meaning, shifts tone, or goes passive.
Longer is rarely better — concise, direct options usually win; distrust padded phrasing.
Convert passive padding to active: 'was decided by the committee' → 'the committee decided'.
Beware choices that 'sound right' (comma splices, 'between you and I') but break a rule.
Compare like with like and completely: 'larger than Spain's', not 'larger than Spain'.
Combine choppy sentences with subordination; split run-ons; keep the main idea in the main clause.
Pick connectors by logic: however/nonetheless = contrast; therefore = result; moreover = addition.
The 'no change / no error' option is sometimes correct — do not invent an error.

flashcards.front: What is the fixed error-scan order?
flashcards.back: Agreement, pronoun (case + reference), tense, modifier, parallelism, comparison, idiom.

flashcards.front: Identify the error: 'The list of demands were rejected.'
flashcards.back: 'were' → 'was'. Subject is 'list' (singular); 'of demands' is just a modifier.

flashcards.front: Five reasons to eliminate a 'best revision' choice
flashcards.back: It adds an error, is wordy/redundant, changes meaning, shifts tone, or is needlessly passive.

flashcards.front: Trap: 'longer is better'
flashcards.back: False. Padding ('due to the fact that', 'in order to') disguises weaker choices; concise usually wins.

flashcards.front: Revise: 'A decision was made by the panel to delay.'
flashcards.back: 'The panel decided to delay.' Active, concise — removes passive padding.

flashcards.front: Why is 'between you and I' wrong?
flashcards.back: After a preposition use object case: 'between you and me'. It sounds right but is incorrect.

flashcards.front: Fix the comparison: 'Our exports exceed Germany.'
flashcards.back: 'exceed Germany's' (compare exports to exports), or 'those of Germany'. Compare like with like.

flashcards.front: Fix the modifier: 'Driving to the summit, the briefing was reviewed.'
flashcards.back: 'Driving to the summit, we reviewed the briefing.' The opening modifier must attach to a person.

flashcards.front: Combine: 'The vote failed. The session ended.'
flashcards.back: Subordinate the lesser idea: 'After the vote failed, the session ended.'

flashcards.front: Connector logic: contrast vs. result vs. addition
flashcards.back: Contrast = however/nevertheless/nonetheless; result = therefore/thus/consequently; addition = moreover/furthermore.

flashcards.front: Fix the parallelism: 'tasked with research, to draft, and analysis.'
flashcards.back: Make forms match: 'tasked with research, drafting, and analysis.'

flashcards.front: When is 'No change' / 'No error' the right answer?
flashcards.back: When every alternative introduces an error or harms the sentence. Don't manufacture a problem.

flashcards.front: Sequence-of-tense fix: 'By the time we arrived, the talks ended.'
flashcards.back: '...the talks had ended.' Use past perfect for the earlier of two past actions.

flashcards.front: Pronoun reference trap: 'The minister told the aide that he was wrong.'
flashcards.back: Ambiguous 'he'. Revise: 'The minister told the aide, "You are wrong",' or name the person.

title: Mechanics: Capitalization, Numbers, Quotation & Formatting
summary: The mechanical conventions—capitalization, number style, quotation placement, dashes, brackets, abbreviations, and list formatting—that signal a careful editor and recur on editing tests.
content:
Mechanics questions test conventions, not ear. Learn the rules cold.

Capitalization:
• Capitalize proper nouns and a title only when it directly precedes a name: "Ambassador Reyes spoke" but "the ambassador spoke." "President Lee" but "the president agreed."
• Capitalize a region but not a compass direction: "economies in the South" (region) vs. "drive south for an hour" (direction).
• Capitalize the major words in document and treaty titles: "the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons," "the Vienna Convention."
• Government bodies as proper names are capitalized: "the Department of State," "the Security Council," but "a government department" generically.

Numbers:
• Spell out whole numbers one through nine (or, in some styles, one through ten) and use numerals for larger: "three delegates," "27 states." Be consistent within a passage.
• Always spell a number that begins a sentence: "Twenty-seven states signed," not "27 states signed."
• Use numerals with percent, and the word in formal prose: "a 4 percent rise" (or '4%' in tables).
• Dates and times: "21 June 2026" or "June 21, 2026"; "9 a.m.," "3:30 p.m." Use an en dash for ranges: "pages 12–18," "2024–2026."

Quotation marks and placement (US convention): periods and commas go inside the closing quotation mark; colons and semicolons go outside; question marks go inside only if the question is part of the quote. "He called it 'a breakthrough,'" but "Did she say 'no'?" Use single quotes for a quote within a quote.

Dashes vs. hyphens: the hyphen (-) joins compounds ("high-level talks"); the en dash (–) spans ranges ("2024–2026"); the em dash (—) sets off an abrupt break or emphatic aside—like this—and takes no surrounding spaces in US style. Do not substitute a hyphen for an em dash.

Parentheses, brackets, ellipses: parentheses ( ) enclose a writer's own aside; square brackets [ ] enclose an editor's insertion into a quotation: "They [the delegates] agreed." Use an ellipsis (. . .) to mark omitted words in a quotation; do not use it as decorative trailing punctuation.

Abbreviations and italics: spell out an acronym on first use with the short form in parentheses—"the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA)"—then use the acronym. Use "U.S."/"US" consistently. Italicize titles of books, newspapers, and treaties-as-published-works and foreign terms not yet anglicized.

Lists and bullets: keep every item grammatically parallel and punctuate consistently—either all items are fragments with no terminal period, or all are full sentences with periods. Introduce a vertical list with a colon only after a complete clause.
keyPoints: Capitalize a title only before a name: 'Ambassador Reyes' vs. 'the ambassador'.
Capitalize regions ('the South') but not directions ('head south'); capitalize official body names ('the Security Council').
Spell out one–nine, use numerals for 10+, and always spell a number that opens a sentence.
Use an en dash for ranges (2024–2026, pages 12–18); never a hyphen.
US quotation rule: periods and commas inside the quotes; colons and semicolons outside.
Hyphen joins compounds; en dash spans ranges; em dash sets off an aside—no spaces in US style.
Square brackets mark an editor's insertion in a quotation; parentheses mark the writer's own aside.
Spell out an acronym on first use with the abbreviation in parentheses, then use the short form.
Italicize titles of books/papers and not-yet-anglicized foreign terms.
Keep list items parallel and punctuate them consistently; introduce a list with a colon after a full clause.

flashcards.front: Capitalize? 'the President / President Lee announced'
flashcards.back: 'President Lee' (title before a name) is capitalized; 'the president announced' is lowercase.

flashcards.front: Capitalize? 'the south of the country' vs. 'tensions in the ___'
flashcards.back: Direction = lowercase ('the south of the country'); region = capital ('tensions in the South').

flashcards.front: Spell out or numeral: '___ states signed; the talks drew ___ observers.'
flashcards.back: Spell one–nine, numerals for 10+: 'nine states', '27 observers'. Be consistent.

flashcards.front: Fix: '27 states ratified the accord.' (sentence start)
flashcards.back: 'Twenty-seven states ratified the accord.' Always spell a number that begins a sentence.

flashcards.front: En dash or hyphen for '2024-2026'?
flashcards.back: En dash: '2024–2026'. The en dash spans ranges; the hyphen joins compound words.

flashcards.front: Em dash spacing in US style
flashcards.back: No spaces around it—like this—and it sets off an abrupt break or emphatic aside.

flashcards.front: US placement: comma/period with closing quote
flashcards.back: Inside: He called it 'progress,' and left. Periods and commas always go inside the quotes.

flashcards.front: US placement: colon/semicolon and question mark with quotes
flashcards.back: Colons/semicolons go outside the quote. Question mark inside only if the question is quoted.

flashcards.front: Parentheses vs. square brackets
flashcards.back: Parentheses = the writer's own aside. Square brackets = an editor's insertion into a quotation: '[the delegates]'.

flashcards.front: How to introduce an acronym
flashcards.back: Spell it out first with the short form in parentheses: 'International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA)', then use 'IAEA'.

flashcards.front: What does an ellipsis (. . .) mark?
flashcards.back: Omitted words within a quotation — not decorative trailing dots in formal prose.

flashcards.front: Percent in formal prose vs. tables
flashcards.back: Formal prose: '4 percent' (numeral + word). Tables/technical: '4%'.

flashcards.front: Italics: when?
flashcards.back: Titles of books/newspapers/published treaties and foreign terms not yet anglicized.

flashcards.front: List punctuation rule
flashcards.back: Keep items parallel; punctuate consistently (all fragments, no periods OR all sentences with periods). Colon only after a full clause.

title: Reading for Meaning, Organization & Coherence
summary: How to find the main idea, judge paragraph organization and flow, use cohesion devices, separate fact from opinion, summarize faithfully, and pick the sentence that best develops a paragraph.
content:
Reading-and-organization items test whether you can see how a passage is built and make it cohere. The skills mirror good writing in reverse.

Finding the main idea: the thesis is the single claim everything else supports — often (not always) in the first or last sentence. To test a candidate main idea, ask whether every other sentence explains, proves, or illustrates it. A statement that only one sentence supports is a detail, not the main idea. The topic sentence of a paragraph does the same job at paragraph scale.

Paragraph unity: every sentence in a paragraph should advance one idea. A sentence that introduces a new topic breaks unity and is usually the 'sentence that does NOT belong' answer. "Which sentence should be removed?" items reward spotting the off-topic intruder.

Logical flow and ordering: ideas should follow a discernible order — chronological (events in time), order of importance (strongest point first or last), general-to-specific (claim then evidence), or problem-then-solution. "Which sentence should come first/where does sentence 3 belong?" items are solved by finding the order that keeps references and logic intact: a sentence that says "this approach" must come after the approach is named.

Cohesion devices tie sentences together: (1) pronoun reference — "The council met. It then voted." — every pronoun must point unambiguously to one earlier noun; (2) deliberate repetition of a key term or a synonym; (3) transitions that signal the relationship (however, therefore, in addition, for instance). A passage feels disjointed when these are missing and over-engineered when every sentence opens with a connective.

Fact vs. opinion and tone: a fact can be verified ("The treaty was signed in 1968"); an opinion expresses judgment ("The treaty was a triumph"). Diplomatic and test prose prizes a neutral, measured tone; words like "disastrous," "brilliant," or "obviously" signal opinion and a loaded tone. Identify the author's stance by these markers, and distinguish the author's view from views the author merely reports.

Summarizing accurately: a good summary captures the main idea and the key supporting points in proportion, in your own words, and adds nothing. The wrong summary choices typically (1) seize on a minor detail, (2) overstate with absolute words ("all," "never," "proves"), (3) introduce information not in the passage, or (4) reverse the author's stance.

Choosing the best-fit sentence: when asked which sentence best completes or develops a paragraph, pick the one that matches the paragraph's topic, maintains its tone, and connects logically to the sentences on either side — not merely a true statement, but the one that fits.
keyPoints: The main idea is the one claim every other sentence supports; test candidates by that standard.
A topic sentence states the paragraph's single controlling idea; unity means every sentence advances it.
To remove a sentence, find the one that introduces a different topic — the off-topic intruder.
Order ideas logically: chronological, order of importance, general-to-specific, or problem-solution.
Solve ordering items by keeping references intact — 'this/such' must follow what it refers to.
Cohesion comes from clear pronoun reference, key-term repetition, and logical transitions.
Fact can be verified; opinion expresses judgment — watch for loaded words ('disastrous', 'obviously').
Separate the author's own stance from views the author merely reports.
A faithful summary keeps proportion, adds nothing, and avoids absolute overstatements.
The best-fit sentence matches topic, tone, and the logic of its neighbors — not just any true statement.

flashcards.front: How do you confirm a sentence is the main idea?
flashcards.back: Check that every other sentence explains, proves, or illustrates it. If only one sentence supports it, it's a detail.

flashcards.front: What is paragraph unity?
flashcards.back: Every sentence advances one controlling idea. A sentence on a different topic breaks unity.

flashcards.front: 'Which sentence should be removed?' — what are you hunting for?
flashcards.back: The off-topic intruder — the sentence that introduces an idea unrelated to the paragraph's topic.

flashcards.front: Four common ways to order ideas
flashcards.back: Chronological, order of importance, general-to-specific, and problem-then-solution.

flashcards.front: How do you place a sentence that begins 'This approach...'?
flashcards.back: After the approach is named — the reference must have an antecedent earlier in the passage.

flashcards.front: Three main cohesion devices
flashcards.back: Clear pronoun reference, deliberate repetition of key terms, and logical transitions.

flashcards.front: Fact vs. opinion
flashcards.back: A fact can be verified ('signed in 1968'); an opinion expresses judgment ('was a triumph').

flashcards.front: Words that signal opinion / loaded tone
flashcards.back: disastrous, brilliant, obviously, clearly, best/worst — evaluative language reveals stance, not fact.

flashcards.front: Author's view vs. reported view
flashcards.back: Distinguish what the author argues from views the author merely cites or attributes to others.

flashcards.front: Four marks of a wrong summary
flashcards.back: Fixates on a minor detail, overstates with absolutes, adds outside information, or reverses the author's stance.

flashcards.front: What makes a summary faithful?
flashcards.back: It states the main idea and key points in proportion, in your own words, adding nothing new.

flashcards.front: How do you pick the best-fit sentence for a paragraph?
flashcards.back: Match topic, tone, and the logic of the surrounding sentences — fit matters more than mere truth.

flashcards.front: Where is the thesis usually found?
flashcards.back: Often the first or last sentence — but verify by the support test, since it can appear elsewhere.

flashcards.front: Why can over-using transitions hurt coherence?
flashcards.back: Opening every sentence with a connective feels mechanical; transitions should mark real logical shifts.
